request_summary: Decompose Hero Academy MVP system map into capability-level child issues for BC-01 (Auth), BC-02 (Child Portal), BC-03 (Parent Portal), BC-04 (Assessment Engine), BC-05 (Gamification), BC-06 (Admin Portal), and CC-01 (Infrastructure). 4-8 child slices per context. MVP scope: React + TS SPA, Node/Express API, SQLite, JWT auth.

items.title: Parent registration and email verification flow
items.outcome: A parent can register with email and password, receive a verification email, and confirm their account before being able to log in.
scope: POST /api/v1/auth/parent/register: validate email format and password strength
Generate verification token (JWT, 24h expiry)
Send verification email with token link
POST /api/v1/auth/parent/verify: confirm token and mark account as verified
Store parent record with hashed password (bcrypt) and verified flag
Prevent login until verified (if OQ-3 decides verification is blocking)
non_goals: Email resend logic (defer if low priority)
acceptance_criteria: POST /register accepts valid email/password, generates verification token, sends email
POST /verify with valid token marks account verified and allows login
POST /verify with expired/invalid token returns 400
Duplicate email registration returns 409 Conflict
Password validation enforces minimum length (12 chars recommended) and complexity
assumptions: Email verification is synchronous (OQ-3 must decide blocking vs non-blocking; assume blocking for MVP security posture)
Email service is available (nodemailer or SendGrid stub)
bcrypt cost factor set to 12 for reasonable security/performance balance
items.split_rationale: Parent registration has distinct API contract and token lifecycle; separating from login/password reset allows focused testing and credential rotation.

reasons: Clear API boundary; email service integration is straightforward; no cross-cutting complexity

items.title: Parent login, logout, and password recovery
items.outcome: A verified parent can log in with email and password, receive access and refresh tokens, log out securely, and reset a forgotten password.
scope: POST /api/v1/auth/parent/login: validate credentials, issue JWT access token (15 min) and refresh token (7 day HttpOnly cookie, rotated on use)
POST /api/v1/auth/parent/logout: revoke refresh token in DB, clear HttpOnly cookie
POST /api/v1/auth/parent/refresh: validate refresh token, issue new access/refresh pair, mark old token as spent
POST /api/v1/auth/parent/forgot-password: send reset link with one-time token (24h expiry)
POST /api/v1/auth/parent/reset-password: validate token, update password, invalidate all existing refresh tokens for account
Refresh token revocation list stored in DB (tokens table with revoked_at timestamp)
non_goals: Account lockout after failed attempts (separate rate-limit concern)
acceptance_criteria: POST /login with valid credentials returns access token in body and refresh token in HttpOnly cookie
POST /login with invalid credentials returns 401 Unauthorized
POST /refresh with valid refresh token returns new access/refresh pair and revokes old refresh token
POST /logout invalidates refresh token in DB and clears cookie
POST /forgot-password sends reset email with valid token
POST /reset-password with valid token updates password and revokes all refresh tokens
assumptions: Refresh tokens are short-lived HttpOnly cookies (rotated on every access token refresh)
Password reset flow does not re-verify email (one-time token is sufficient)
Refresh token revocation is checked in middleware before accessing protected resources
items.split_rationale: Login and session management are distinct from registration; separating allows focused testing of token rotation and revocation mechanics.

reasons: Token lifecycle is well-defined; revocation logic is straightforward; no frontend complexity

items.title: Child PIN authentication and avatar selection
items.outcome: A child can enter a 4-digit PIN and select an avatar, receive a short-lived JWT in memory, and be logged in without exposing the PIN to localStorage.
scope: POST /api/v1/auth/child/login: validate PIN (4 digits), return JWT access token (15 min, no refresh token)
Child JWT claim: { child_id, parent_id, hero_name, avatar_id } — no email, no PII
PIN rate-limiting: 5 failed attempts per child per 10 minutes (IP + child_id)
Return JWT response with explicit instruction to store in memory only (no localStorage)
Avatar selection UI: display 6-8 avatar options from config (OQ-5 schema must be specified)
Client-side: store JWT in closure/memory variable, not localStorage or sessionStorage
non_goals: Parent approval workflow for child login
Child account creation (owned by Parent Portal BC-03)
acceptance_criteria: POST /login accepts valid 4-digit PIN and returns JWT access token with 15 min expiry
POST /login with invalid PIN increments attempt counter
After 5 failed attempts within 10 min, subsequent attempts are rate-limited (429 Too Many Requests)
Child JWT payload excludes email, surname, or other PII
JWT includes parent_id for subscription gating lookups
Avatar selection renders available avatars from configuration
assumptions: Avatar configuration is a JSON file (path and dimensions TBD in OQ-5)
PIN is stored as bcrypt hash in the child record
Rate-limiting is keyed by child_id and request source IP
Child JWT is intentionally short-lived (15 min) and carries no refresh token to reduce exposure
items.split_rationale: Child auth is fundamentally different from parent auth (no email, no refresh token, in-memory JWT); separating it preserves the distinct security model.

reasons: Simpler than parent auth (no email verification, no refresh); rate-limiting is a known pattern

items.title: Admin login and role authorization
items.outcome: An admin can log in with email and password and receive an access token with the admin role claim; all admin routes are guarded by role middleware.
scope: POST /api/v1/auth/admin/login: validate admin email and password, issue JWT access token (15 min) with role: 'admin'
Admin records seeded in DB via seed script (OQ-8 — no invite flow for MVP)
Admin JWT claim: { admin_id, email, role: 'admin' }
Middleware requireRole('admin'): validates JWT and checks role claim before passing to route handler
Apply middleware to all /api/v1/admin/* routes
No HttpOnly refresh token for admin (access-token-only for MVP simplicity)
non_goals: Admin account creation UI or invite workflow (OQ-8 — scope TBD)
acceptance_criteria: POST /login accepts admin email/password and returns JWT with admin role
POST /login with invalid credentials returns 401
Requests to /api/v1/admin/* without valid admin JWT return 401 or 403
requireRole('admin') middleware correctly validates role claim
Admin logout is stateless (no token revocation needed for MVP)
assumptions: Admin accounts are seeded via script only; no self-signup or invite flow in MVP (OQ-8)
Admin role is checked only on route entry; no nested role escalation needed
Admin JWT is short-lived (15 min) and access-token-only for simplicity
items.split_rationale: Admin auth is simpler than parent (no refresh token, no verification) and warrants its own capability for clarity and focused testing.

reasons: Straightforward email/password flow; role middleware is a standard pattern; no token refresh complexity

items.title: JWT token generation, validation, and middleware
items.outcome: The system can issue, sign, and validate JWTs consistently across all auth flows; middleware enforces role-based access on protected routes.
scope: JWT signing: HS256 algorithm, RS256 if key rotation is required post-MVP
Token claims structure: { sub, iat, exp, role, [parent_id], [child_id], [admin_id] }
Middleware verifyToken(): extract Bearer token from Authorization header, validate signature and expiry
Middleware requireAuth(): apply verifyToken() and return 401 if invalid
Middleware requireRole(role): apply verifyToken() and check role claim; return 403 if role mismatch
Error responses: 401 Unauthorized (missing/invalid token), 403 Forbidden (insufficient role)
non_goals: Key rotation (can be added post-MVP if using RS256)
acceptance_criteria: Issued JWT is valid and verifiable by middleware
JWT signature validation fails if secret is incorrect
Expired JWT returns 401
verifyToken() correctly extracts claims
requireAuth() blocks unauthenticated requests with 401
requireRole() blocks requests from wrong role with 403
assumptions: JWT secret is stored in environment variable (12.random() or similar, not hardcoded)
HS256 is sufficient for single-server deployment; RS256 can be added if multi-server auth is needed post-MVP
Token expiry is enforced by middleware, not client-side
items.split_rationale: JWT infrastructure is used by all three auth flows (parent, child, admin); separating it ensures consistent issuance/validation and reduces duplication.

reasons: Clear token structure; middleware is a standard Express pattern; no novel crypto logic

items.title: Refresh token generation, rotation, and HttpOnly cookie handling
items.outcome: Parent refresh tokens are issued in HttpOnly cookies, rotated on each access token refresh, and revoked on logout or account suspension.
scope: Refresh token generation: longer JWT (7 day expiry) stored in HttpOnly, Secure, SameSite=Strict cookie
POST /api/v1/auth/parent/refresh: validate cookie-based refresh token, issue new access token + new refresh token (rotation), revoke old refresh token
Refresh token revocation: mark old token in revocation list with timestamp; revoked tokens fail validation
Revocation check in middleware: verifyToken() queries DB for revoked tokens before accepting
Cookie naming: __refreshToken (prevents accidental JS access)
Secure flag ensures cookie is sent only over HTTPS in production
non_goals: Multiple refresh tokens per account (one valid at a time)
Refresh token reuse detection beyond standard rotation
acceptance_criteria: Refresh token is issued in HttpOnly cookie with Secure and SameSite flags
POST /refresh issues new access token and new refresh token, invalidates old token
POST /refresh with revoked refresh token returns 401
Revocation check prevents replay of old tokens
Cookie is sent by browser automatically; response sets new cookie
HttpOnly flag prevents JS access to token
assumptions: Single valid refresh token per parent account at any time (rotation, not accumulation)
Revocation list is queried on every refresh request (acceptable for MVP scale)
HTTPS is enforced in production; Secure flag will be set conditionally
items.split_rationale: Refresh token rotation and revocation are distinct from JWT issuance and from password reset; separating clarifies the token lifecycle.

reasons: Standard OAuth refresh token pattern; HttpOnly cookie handling is well-understood

items.title: PIN attempt rate-limiting and brute-force protection
items.outcome: Child PIN login is protected from brute-force attacks by enforcing a 5-attempt limit per 10-minute window per child.
scope: Rate-limit key: (child_id, request_source_IP) composite
Limit: 5 failed attempts per 10 minutes
On 6th attempt within window: return 429 Too Many Requests with Retry-After header
Store attempt count in memory cache (Redis optional for MVP; in-memory is acceptable for single server)
Reset counter on successful login or after 10-minute window expires
Log rate-limit events for monitoring (optional: feed to admin dashboard later)
non_goals: Account lockout (rate-limit is temporary, not permanent)
acceptance_criteria: 5 failed PIN attempts within 10 min are accepted
6th failed attempt within 10 min returns 429 Too Many Requests
Retry-After header indicates seconds until next attempt allowed
Successful login resets attempt counter
Counter expires after 10 minutes of no activity
Rate-limit is keyed per child + per IP
assumptions: In-memory rate-limit state is acceptable for single-server MVP (no cross-server coordination needed)
Child PIN is short (4 digits); 5 attempts is a reasonable threshold before lockout
IP-based tracking is a reasonable proxy for preventing mass attacks from single source
items.split_rationale: Rate-limiting is a security concern orthogonal to PIN validation; separating allows focused testing and potential migration to Redis later.

reasons: Straightforward counter logic; can use in-memory cache for MVP scale

items.title: Role-based access control middleware and route protection
items.outcome: All protected routes enforce role-based access control; parent-only, child-only, and admin-only endpoints return 403 if the wrong role attempts access.
scope: Middleware stack: verifyToken() → requireRole() → route handler
Role constants: ROLE.PARENT, ROLE.CHILD, ROLE.ADMIN
requireRole(expectedRole) middleware: checks JWT role claim, returns 403 if mismatch
Guard all /api/v1/parent/* routes with requireRole('parent')
Guard all /api/v1/child/* routes with requireRole('child')
Guard all /api/v1/admin/* routes with requireRole('admin')
Error responses include WWW-Authenticate header for HTTP spec compliance
non_goals: Fine-grained resource ownership checks (e.g., parent can only view own children)
acceptance_criteria: Child JWT attempting parent route returns 403 Forbidden
Parent JWT attempting admin route returns 403 Forbidden
Middleware is applied consistently to all role-specific routes
Unauthenticated request to protected route returns 401, not 403
Error response includes WWW-Authenticate header
assumptions: Role-based access control (RBAC) is sufficient for MVP; no attribute-based or resource-level permissions
Each request has exactly one role (no multi-role users in MVP)
items.split_rationale: Role-based access control is a separate concern from token validation; separating allows focused testing of authorization logic.

reasons: Straightforward middleware pattern; RBAC is well-established

items.title: Child login UI: PIN entry and avatar selection
items.outcome: A child can enter a 4-digit PIN using numeric keypad (mobile-friendly), select an avatar from a displayed gallery, and log in to access the child portal.
scope: PIN input screen: numeric keypad with visual feedback (4 circles, masked entry)
PIN submission: POST to /api/v1/auth/child/login, handle 429 rate-limit response, display 'Too many attempts' message
Avatar selection screen: display 6-8 avatars with names, allow selection before or after PIN entry (design decision)
Error handling: display 'Invalid PIN' message on 401, respect rate-limit backoff
Store JWT in memory variable (not localStorage); expose JWT to outgoing requests via interceptor
Touch targets ≥ 44px; font ≥ 18px for young children
Mobile-first: 375px+ viewport support, no horizontal scroll
non_goals: Child account creation (owned by Parent Portal)
acceptance_criteria: Numeric keypad accepts PIN input and visually masks entry
PIN submission calls API and displays result
Rate-limit 429 response triggers backoff message (try again in X seconds)
Avatar gallery renders 6-8 selectable avatars with labels
JWT is stored in memory (verify in DevTools that localStorage is not used)
Touch targets are ≥ 44px; font is readable on mobile
No horizontal scroll on 375px viewport
assumptions: Avatar config JSON is available at build time (OQ-5 schema must be specified)
Numeric keypad is custom React component or mobile browser native input type=tel
Young children (8-10) need larger touch targets and clearer visual feedback than typical adult apps
items.split_rationale: Child login UI is distinct from the mission runner and profile UI; separating allows focused development and testing of the onboarding flow.

reasons: Frontend-only; clear API boundary; standard form+gallery patterns

items.title: City map and zone navigation
items.outcome: The child can view a stylized city map of Mathropolis with zone/district unlocking rules applied, navigate between unlocked and locked zones, and see unlocking progress.
scope: Fetch city map config and child progress from API: GET /api/v1/child/progress
Render Mathropolis city with 5 districts, apply visual unlock states based on XP thresholds (e.g., 'locked', 'unlocked', 'completing')
Click/tap zone → navigate to district view
Locked zone shows unlock requirement (e.g., '50 XP to unlock') with progress bar
Zoom and pan interaction (Framer Motion or Konva for canvas)
Fly-in animation on city load (Framer Motion)
Mobile: swipe to pan, pinch to zoom (optional, or static view)
Responsive: full-screen map on mobile, side-by-side on tablet
acceptance_criteria: Fetch child progress updates map unlock state
Locked zone displays unlock requirement and progress bar
Click zone navigates to district view (routing)
Map is interactive: zoom, pan, or tap to select
Fly-in animation plays on initial load
Responsive on 375px and 768px viewports
No horizontal scroll on mobile
assumptions: City map is a fixed 2D layout (no procedural generation)
Unlock thresholds are based on XP milestones (exact thresholds TBD)
Framer Motion is available for animations
items.split_rationale: City map navigation is a distinct UX layer from mission execution; separating allows independent animation and interaction testing.

reasons: Requires animation layer (Framer Motion) and interactive map rendering, but well-scoped to navigation and unlock display

items.title: District view and mission selection
items.outcome: After selecting a district, the child can view 3 available missions with difficulty and completion status, and select a mission to start.
scope: Fetch district details from API: GET /api/v1/child/district/:id
Render district name, flavor text, and mission list
Each mission card shows: title, difficulty (1-3 stars), completion status (not started / in progress / completed)
Show average score on completed missions (or similar progress indicator)
Click mission → navigate to mission runner
Back button returns to city map
Difficulty stars are visual; no gameplay impact in MVP
non_goals: Mission prerequisites or blocking (all missions in a district are available)
Video or audio instructions per mission
acceptance_criteria: Fetch district returns mission list with status and difficulty
Mission cards render title, difficulty stars, and completion status
Click mission navigates to mission runner with mission ID
Back button navigates to city map
Responsive on mobile and tablet
No horizontal scroll
assumptions: All missions in a district are unlocked once the district is unlocked (no individual mission prerequisites)
Difficulty and status are read-only attributes; no editing needed
Each district has exactly 3 missions (MVP scope: Mathropolis only)
items.split_rationale: District and mission selection are a distinct navigation layer; separating from mission execution allows focused testing of the selection UI.

reasons: Simple list and card rendering; standard routing patterns

items.title: Mission runner: question rendering and answer capture
items.outcome: The child can view a question with 4 multiple-choice answers, select an answer, and submit their attempt.
scope: Fetch mission questions from API: GET /api/v1/child/mission/:id/attempt (returns ordered question set, attempt UUID)
Render question stem and 4 answer options as tappable buttons (44px+ height)
Visual feedback on selection (highlight, color change)
Submit button to lock in answer and move to next question
Question counter (e.g., '1 of 10')
Back/quit option (warn before abandoning attempt)
Store attempt UUID and answers in client state
Mobile-first: full-width buttons, large font (18px+)
acceptance_criteria: Fetch mission returns 10 questions and unique attempt UUID
Questions render with stem and 4 answer options
Answer buttons are ≥ 44px height and respond to tap
Selected answer is visually highlighted
Submit button advances to next question
Question counter displays current position (e.g., '3 of 10')
Back button prompts user before abandoning
Attempt UUID and answers are stored in client state
assumptions: 10 questions per mission (MVP scope)
Multiple-choice only (no essay or numeric answers in MVP)
No time limit per question or mission
Randomization of answer option order is TBD (OQ-6)
items.split_rationale: Question rendering and answer capture are distinct from submission and feedback; separating allows independent testing of the UI and state management.

reasons: Straightforward form rendering; standard state management for answer tracking

items.title: Mission submission and feedback UI with animations
items.outcome: After answering all 10 questions, the child can submit their attempt, see animated feedback (correct/incorrect per question), and view their score and XP earned.
scope: Submit attempt: POST /api/v1/child/attempt with attempt UUID and answers
Receive scored response: { stars: 1-3, xp_earned, feedback: { question_id, correct_answer, is_correct } }
Animation sequence: submit button → loading spinner → reveal correct/incorrect per question → score reveal → XP popup
Correct answer: green checkmark + brief positive message
Incorrect answer: red X + show correct answer
Final score screen: stars (1-3), XP earned, total missions completed, streak bonus (if applicable)
Next button: return to district view or mission selection
Use Framer Motion for all animations (stagger, scale, fade)
non_goals: Detailed explanations of why answers are correct
acceptance_criteria: POST /attempt with answers returns scored response
Animation sequence plays for all 10 questions with visual feedback (correct/incorrect)
Stars and XP earned are displayed prominently
Streak bonus is shown if applicable (depends on BC-05 completion)
Next button returns to district view
Framer Motion animations are smooth and not jarring
Mobile: animations scale appropriately for 375px viewport
assumptions: Scoring is handled by Assessment Engine (BC-04); UI receives stars and XP
Animations are core to engagement for this age group (8-10 year olds)
Framer Motion stagger and scale animations are used for visual interest
items.split_rationale: Feedback and animations are distinct from question capture; separating allows focused testing of the result UX and animation performance.

reasons: Animation layer adds complexity, but well-scoped to feedback reveal sequence

items.title: Hero profile display: avatar, stats, and achievements
items.outcome: The child can view their hero profile with avatar, current XP and rank, missions completed, streaks, and earned achievements.
scope: Fetch child stats from API: GET /api/v1/child/profile
Render hero profile card: avatar, hero name, rank badge, XP bar (visual progress to next rank)
Stats summary: missions completed, average score, current streak, strongest/weakest district
Achievements/badges section: display earned badges with names and unlock dates
Visual polish: gradients, hero theme styling, rank-specific colors
Mobile-first: full-width cards, large readable text
Back button returns to mission selection or city map
acceptance_criteria: Fetch profile returns all hero stats and achievements
Profile card renders avatar, name, rank badge, and XP bar
XP bar visually represents progress to next rank
Stats are displayed: missions, average score, streak, strongest/weakest
Achievements are displayed with names and dates
Mobile: no horizontal scroll, readable font sizes
Visual polish matches superhero theme
assumptions: Hero stats are computed by Gamification service (BC-05) and exposed via API
Achievements are badge objects with names, descriptions, and unlock dates
Rank badge styling is theme-consistent (colors, icons)
items.split_rationale: Profile display is a distinct view from the mission runner; separating allows independent styling and stat presentation.

reasons: Static display of data; standard card and stat rendering

items.title: Client-side authentication state and session management
items.outcome: The child's JWT is stored securely in memory, included in all API requests, and the session is automatically logged out after 2 hours of inactivity.
scope: JWT storage: in-memory variable (closure or Context API), not localStorage or sessionStorage
API interceptor: inject JWT into Authorization: Bearer header for all /api/v1/child/* requests
Idle timer: reset on every user interaction (tap, keystroke, scroll); log out after 2 hours (7200s) of inactivity
Log out: clear JWT from memory, remove any auth headers, redirect to child login screen
Handle 401 responses: refresh token flow (not applicable for child JWT, so log out immediately)
Error boundary: catch auth-related API errors and display appropriate user message
non_goals: Background refresh of JWT (child token does not refresh)
acceptance_criteria: JWT is stored only in memory (verify DevTools shows no localStorage entries)
API requests include Authorization: Bearer header with JWT
Idle timer logs out child after 7200s of inactivity
Manual logout clears JWT and redirects to login
401 response triggers immediate logout
Error messages are user-friendly (no token details exposed)
assumptions: Child JWT is short-lived (15 min) and access-token-only (no refresh token)
Idle timeout of 2 hours is reasonable for occasional child access
In-memory storage is acceptable; no cross-tab sync needed for MVP
items.split_rationale: Client-side session management is distinct from server-side auth logic; separating allows focused testing of token handling and idle timeout.

reasons: Straightforward Context API or closure-based storage; standard interceptor pattern

items.title: Mobile-first responsive design and accessibility baseline
items.outcome: All child portal screens are responsive, accessible, and optimized for 8-10 year olds on mobile (375px+) and tablet (768px+) devices.
scope: Base responsive layout: CSS Grid or Flexbox, no horizontal scroll on 375px+
Touch targets: ≥ 44px height and width for all interactive elements
Font sizes: ≥ 18px for body text, ≥ 24px for headings (larger than typical adult apps)
Color contrast: WCAG AA (4.5:1 for text); no color-only indicators (e.g., red/green must have icon/text)
Keyboard navigation: all interactive elements are reachable via Tab (not required for children using touch, but good practice)
Screen reader support: semantic HTML, alt text for images, ARIA labels where needed
Viewport meta tag: width=device-width, initial-scale=1, viewport-fit=cover
No horizontal scroll at any breakpoint (375px, 768px, 1024px+)
Test on real mobile devices: iOS Safari, Android Chrome (if feasible)
acceptance_criteria: All screens render correctly at 375px, 768px, and 1024px without horizontal scroll
Touch targets are ≥ 44px on all interactive elements
Font sizes are ≥ 18px for body text, ≥ 24px for headings
Color contrast meets WCAG AA (checked with automated tool or manual review)
Semantic HTML is used (buttons, inputs, headings, landmarks)
Images have descriptive alt text
Tab navigation works for keyboard users
App tested on iOS Safari and Android Chrome
assumptions: Primary device is mobile (375px+); tablet (768px+) is secondary
Touch input is primary; keyboard navigation is nice-to-have
8-10 year olds benefit from larger touch targets and text (larger than typical adult apps)
Testing on real devices is ideal but not required for MVP (emulators acceptable)
items.split_rationale: Responsive design and accessibility are cross-cutting concerns that span all child portal screens; treating as a separate capability ensures consistent baseline.

reasons: Applies to all 7 BC-02 child capabilities; should be validated in context of each
May require multiple passes: initial build → accessibility audit → refinement

items.title: Parent registration and email verification
items.outcome: A parent can register with their email and password, verify their email address, and create their account to access the parent portal.
scope: POST /api/v1/auth/parent/register endpoint (already owned by BC-01-C1, but UI is in BC-03)
Registration form: email, password, confirm password, name (optional for MVP)
Submit registration → receive verification email with link
Verification flow: click email link → confirm token → account is verified
Handle errors: duplicate email, invalid email, password mismatch, weak password
Success: redirect to login page or auto-login (design decision)
Responsive: mobile-first, accessible forms
non_goals: Email resend logic (defer if low priority)
acceptance_criteria: Registration form accepts email and password with validation feedback
POST /register sends verification email
Email verification link contains valid token
Clicking verification link confirms account and allows login
Duplicate email returns error message
Weak password returns validation error
Form is responsive and accessible (WCAG AA)
assumptions: Email verification is blocking (per OQ-3 assumption; if non-blocking, flow is simpler)
Parent name is optional for MVP (focus on email verification first)
items.split_rationale: Registration UI is separate from login UI; allows independent UX iteration and testing.

reasons: Standard registration form; leverages BC-01-C1 API

items.title: Parent login, logout, and password recovery UI
items.outcome: A verified parent can log in with email and password, access their dashboard, and securely recover a forgotten password.
scope: Login form: email and password inputs with remember-me checkbox (optional for MVP)
POST /api/v1/auth/parent/login endpoint (owned by BC-01-C2, but UI is in BC-03)
Handle 401 response: display 'Invalid email or password' message (no user enumeration)
Forgot password link → email input form → send reset email
Reset email link → new password form → POST /api/v1/auth/parent/reset-password
Success: redirect to dashboard or auto-login
Logout: POST /api/v1/auth/parent/logout, clear auth state, redirect to login
non_goals: Account lockout after failed attempts (rate-limited at API level by BC-01)
acceptance_criteria: Login form submits email/password and receives access token
Invalid credentials display generic error (no user enumeration)
Forgot password sends reset email
Reset link contains valid one-time token
Reset password updates account and allows new login
Logout clears auth state and redirects to login
Forms are responsive and accessible
assumptions: Remember-me is optional (not required for MVP)
Rate-limiting is handled at API level (BC-01)
items.split_rationale: Login and recovery flows are distinct from registration and account management; separate UI development and testing.

reasons: Standard login and recovery forms; leverages BC-01-C2 API

items.title: Add and manage child profiles
items.outcome: A parent can add new child profiles to their account, view all their children, edit child names, and soft-delete child accounts.
scope: Child profile fields: child name (hero name), age/grade (optional), PIN (generated or user-provided)
Add child form: name input, PIN input (or generate button), submit
Child list view: table or card grid with child name, PIN (masked for display), average score, last activity
Edit child: update name and PIN; no avatar change (child selects on login)
Delete child: soft-delete with confirmation dialog (archive, not remove)
POST /api/v1/parent/child (create), GET /api/v1/parent/children (list), PUT /api/v1/parent/child/:id (update), DELETE /api/v1/parent/child/:id (soft-delete)
Responsive: table on desktop, cards on mobile
non_goals: Parent approval of child activity (OQ-9 — out of MVP scope)
acceptance_criteria: Add child form submits name and PIN
POST /child returns new child record with ID
Child list displays all children with name, PIN (masked), score, last activity
Edit child updates name and PIN
Delete child soft-deletes with confirmation
Deleted children no longer appear in list (archived)
Responsive on mobile and desktop
assumptions: PIN is user-provided or generated (design decision TBD)
Parent can manage multiple children (no limit in MVP, but scalability should be considered)
Soft-delete prevents accidental data loss; archived children are not visible in normal list
items.split_rationale: Child management is a distinct parent workflow; separate from progress dashboard and account settings.

reasons: Standard CRUD forms; straightforward API integration

items.title: Child progress dashboard and activity metrics
items.outcome: A parent can view each child's progress with missions completed, average score, current rank, streak, and strongest/weakest districts.
scope: Dashboard layout: list or tab view of all children, selectable
Per child: hero name, avatar, rank badge, XP bar (progress to next rank)
Progress stats: missions completed, average score, current streak, longest streak (all-time)
District breakdown: strongest (highest average score) and weakest (lowest average score) districts
Recent activity: last 5 missions with date, score, district (optional for MVP)
GET /api/v1/parent/child/:id/progress endpoint returns all stats
Responsive: single column on mobile, multi-column grid on desktop
non_goals: Detailed mission history (limit to last 5 for MVP)
Comparison with other children or leaderboards
acceptance_criteria: Dashboard lists all children
Selected child displays hero profile (name, avatar, rank, XP bar)
Stats are accurate: missions, average score, streak, strongest/weakest
Recent activity shows last 5 missions
Responsive on mobile and desktop
Data is read-only (parent cannot modify child scores)
assumptions: Child progress data is owned by Assessment Engine and Gamification services
Parent can see all children's progress without approval workflow (OQ-9 is out of scope)
Streak and strongest/weakest are computed by Gamification service
items.split_rationale: Progress dashboard is a distinct parent concern from child management; separate development and testing.

reasons: Data display and summary calculations; straightforward API integration

items.title: Subscription and billing information display
items.outcome: A parent can view their current subscription plan (free for MVP), see what features are included, and understand next steps for premium plans.
scope: Subscription card: plan name (free), price ($0/month for MVP), features list
For MVP: all features are free; no upgrade button
Badge on dashboard: 'Free Plan' or similar
GET /api/v1/parent/subscription endpoint returns plan name and feature list
Text placeholder: 'Premium plans coming soon' or similar (design decision)
Responsive: card view on all devices
acceptance_criteria: Subscription card displays plan name and price
Features list is accurate (all features available in free plan for MVP)
Placeholder for premium plans is present
GET /subscription returns plan data
Responsive on mobile and desktop
assumptions: All MVP users are on free plan (no gating enforcement needed)
Premium plans are a post-MVP feature (placeholder only)
Stripe integration is handled by BC-07 post-MVP
items.split_rationale: Billing information is a distinct parent concern; separating allows independent design and future Stripe integration (BC-07).

reasons: Simple card display for MVP; no transaction logic

items.title: Question bank API: storage, retrieval, and CRUD operations
items.outcome: The system stores questions with metadata (topic, difficulty, stem, answers) and exposes APIs for admin CRUD and child retrieval.
scope: Question model: id, topic_id, subject_id, stem (text), answer_options (JSON array), correct_answer_idx (0-3), difficulty (1-3), created_at, soft_delete_flag
Seed data: 150 questions at 11+ standard across 5 topics in 1 subject (Mathropolis)
Admin CRUD endpoints: POST /api/v1/admin/question, GET /api/v1/admin/question/:id, PUT /api/v1/admin/question/:id, DELETE /api/v1/admin/question/:id (soft-delete)
Child retrieval: GET /api/v1/child/mission/:mission_id/attempt (returns ordered question set filtered for mission)
Schema validation: question stem is non-empty, answer_options array has exactly 4 items, correct_answer_idx is 0-3
Indexing: topic_id and subject_id for efficient filtering
non_goals: Rich text editor for question stem (plain text for MVP)
acceptance_criteria: POST /question creates question and returns ID
GET /question/:id returns question (admin view includes all fields; child view may exclude answers)
PUT /question/:id updates question fields
DELETE /question/:id soft-deletes
Seed script creates 150 questions
Questions are indexed by topic for efficient retrieval
Schema validation prevents invalid records
assumptions: Questions are plain text (no rich formatting in MVP)
4 multiple-choice answers per question (fixed for MVP)
Difficulty is 1-3 star rating (visual; no gameplay impact)
items.split_rationale: Question bank is a data store with dedicated CRUD API; separating from assessment definitions and attempt scoring clarifies responsibilities.

reasons: Straightforward CRUD API; seed script is standard data load

items.title: Assessment definitions API: CRUD and topic-based fetching
items.outcome: The system stores assessment definitions (missions) with metadata and allows admins to create/edit them, and children to fetch assessments by topic.
scope: Assessment model: id, mission_id, name, description, topic_id, district_id, num_questions (10), difficulty (1-3), created_at, soft_delete_flag
Admin CRUD: POST /api/v1/admin/assessment, GET /api/v1/admin/assessment/:id, PUT /api/v1/admin/assessment/:id, DELETE /api/v1/admin/assessment/:id (soft-delete)
Assessment questions: link questions to assessment (many-to-many or array of question IDs in assessment record)
Child retrieval: GET /api/v1/child/assessment?topic_id=X or GET /api/v1/child/mission/:mission_id (returns assessment with num_questions questions)
Seed data: 5 topics, 15 assessments (3 per district, 5 districts), linked to question set
Schema validation: name is non-empty, num_questions matches actual question count, topic_id exists
non_goals: Question randomization per attempt (owned by BC-04-C6)
acceptance_criteria: POST /assessment creates assessment and returns ID
Assessment links to questions (10 per assessment for MVP)
GET /assessment/:id returns assessment with question list
PUT /assessment/:id updates assessment fields
DELETE /assessment/:id soft-deletes
Seed script creates 15 assessments (3 per district)
GET /assessment?topic_id=X returns assessments for topic
Child API returns assessment with exact num_questions questions
assumptions: Each assessment has exactly 10 questions (fixed for MVP)
Difficulty is 1-3 star rating (visual; no gameplay impact)
Assessments are seeded with fixed question sets (no dynamic selection in MVP)
items.split_rationale: Assessment definitions are distinct from question bank and from attempt scoring; clear separation of concerns.

reasons: Straightforward CRUD API; many-to-many linking is a standard pattern

items.title: Attempt submission API and answer evaluation
items.outcome: A child can submit their 10 answers to an assessment, the system evaluates them against the correct answers, and returns a score.
scope: Attempt submission: POST /api/v1/child/attempt with { attempt_uuid, mission_id, answers: [question_id: answer_idx, ...] }
Answer validation: verify all 10 questions are answered, answer_idx is 0-3
Scoring: compare each answer_idx to correct_answer_idx, count correct answers
Response: { attempt_id, mission_id, score: N/10, attempted_at, child_id }
Attempt deduplication: attempt_uuid is unique constraint; replay same UUID returns cached result (idempotency)
Store attempt record: attempt_id, child_id, mission_id, score, answers (JSON), attempted_at, created_at
Trigger post-attempt side-effect: notify Gamification service to compute stars and XP (BC-05)
acceptance_criteria: POST /attempt accepts answers and returns score
Score is computed correctly (correct answers / 10)
All 10 questions must be answered; incomplete attempts return 400
Attempt UUID is unique; replaying same UUID returns cached result (idempotency)
Attempt is stored with child_id and mission_id for later retrieval
Post-attempt hook triggers Gamification side-effect
assumptions: Attempt submission is synchronous and returns result immediately
Idempotency is achieved via unique constraint on attempt_uuid
Post-attempt side-effects run in-process (no async queue for MVP)
items.split_rationale: Attempt submission and scoring are core to the assessment flow; separating from star/XP computation keeps the contract clean.

reasons: Straightforward API; answer comparison is simple logic; idempotency via UUID is a known pattern

items.title: Star rating computation based on score
items.outcome: After an attempt is scored, the system computes a star rating (1-3) based on the child's accuracy and includes it in the feedback response.
scope: Star thresholds (TBD, e.g., ≥ 70% = 1 star, ≥ 80% = 2 stars, ≥ 90% = 3 stars)
Compute stars in POST /attempt response: { attempt_id, score, stars, xp_earned }
Star computation logic: score / num_questions * 100 → check thresholds → return 1-3
All attempts earn at least 1 star (e.g., if score >= 50%, 1 star; >= 75%, 2 stars; >= 90%, 3 stars)
Thresholds are configurable (constants or DB records, TBD)
XP earned is based on stars (e.g., 1 star = 10 XP, 2 stars = 20 XP, 3 stars = 30 XP)
acceptance_criteria: Star thresholds are defined and documented
Score is converted to stars (1-3) correctly
All attempts earn at least 1 star
XP earned is based on stars
POST /attempt response includes stars and xp_earned
Thresholds can be adjusted without code change (ideally)
assumptions: Star thresholds are simple percentage-based rules (≥ X% = Y stars)
XP earned is proportional to stars (fixed multiplier)
Difficulty does not affect star calculation (all missions have same scale)
items.split_rationale: Star computation is a distinct gamification output; separating allows independent testing and potential future complexity (e.g., difficulty-adjusted scores).

reasons: Simple percentage-based threshold logic; no external dependencies

items.title: Subscription-gated access control
items.outcome: Child access to assessment attempts is gated by subscription plan; the system checks the parent's plan before allowing an attempt and returns 403 if plan is not active.
scope: Subscription check in POST /attempt: fetch parent's subscriptions record, verify plan is active (plan != 'free' or allow all in MVP stub)
For MVP stub: all users are on 'free' plan; gate is stubbed to always return 200 (placeholder for post-MVP Stripe gating)
Return 403 Forbidden if parent's plan is 'free' and mission is 'premium' (design: which missions are premium is TBD)
Response: { error: 'subscription_required', upgrade_link: '/parent/upgrade' } (placeholder)
Log gating decisions for admin visibility (optional)
acceptance_criteria: POST /attempt checks parent's subscription plan
Free plan users can attempt free assessments (all MVP assessments)
Premium users can attempt premium assessments (not in MVP; placeholders only)
403 Forbidden is returned if plan check fails
Plan field is read from subscriptions table
All MVP users are on free plan (stubbed)
assumptions: MVP stub: all assessments are 'free'; all users are on 'free' plan; gating is not enforced
Subscription plan is stored in subscriptions table with parent_id foreign key
Post-MVP: Stripe will update plan field on webhook events (handled by BC-07)
items.split_rationale: Subscription gating is a distinct concern that crosses Assessment Engine and Payments domains; separating allows independent testing and future Stripe integration.

reasons: Simple table lookup and comparison; MVP stub is minimal code

items.title: Attempt deduplication via UUID
items.outcome: Attempt submissions are idempotent; a child can safely retry a POST /attempt request with the same UUID and receive the same result without double-scoring.
scope: Client generates attempt_uuid (UUID v4) at mission start and stores it locally
POST /attempt includes attempt_uuid in request body
Database: unique constraint on (child_id, attempt_uuid) composite key
On duplicate submission: query DB for existing attempt with same UUID, return cached result (200 OK, same response as before)
Response headers: include Idempotency-Key or similar to signal retry-safe semantics
Error handling: if duplicate attempt has different answers, log warning and return cached result (conservative approach)
acceptance_criteria: Client generates attempt UUID at mission start
POST /attempt with UUID creates new attempt record
POST /attempt with duplicate UUID returns cached result without re-scoring
Unique constraint on (child_id, attempt_uuid) prevents duplicate inserts
Response includes attempt_id for tracking
assumptions: Client-generated UUID is sufficient for idempotency (no server-side token needed)
Duplicate attempts with different answers should not occur in normal flow; log and return cached result
Single server deployment; distributed idempotency not required
items.split_rationale: Idempotency is a distinct resilience concern; separating allows focused testing of retry scenarios.

reasons: Simple unique constraint and cache lookup; standard idempotency pattern

items.title: XP accumulation and persistence service
items.outcome: After an attempt is scored, the child's XP total is incremented and persisted; the new XP and current rank are returned to the frontend.
scope: Triggered as post-attempt side-effect (called from BC-04-C3)
Update child.xp_total = xp_total + xp_earned in transaction
Persist to child record in DB
Return updated child stats: { xp_total, rank_tier, xp_to_next_rank }
XP is cumulative (never decreases)
Rank is computed from XP (see BC-05-C2)
In-process service (not async/queue for MVP)
non_goals: XP multipliers per district (keep simple for MVP)
acceptance_criteria: POST /attempt triggers XP accumulation
Child.xp_total is incremented correctly
Rank is recomputed after XP change
Updated stats are returned immediately
XP is persisted and survives app restart
assumptions: XP earned is provided by Assessment Engine (stars-based, e.g., 1 star = 10 XP)
Rank is computed separately (BC-05-C2) based on XP thresholds
In-process service runs synchronously within attempt submission
items.split_rationale: XP accumulation is a distinct gamification service; separating from rank evaluation allows independent testing.

reasons: Simple transaction to increment and persist XP; no complex logic

items.title: Rank tier evaluation and milestone transitions
items.outcome: When a child's XP reaches a milestone threshold, their rank tier is incremented and an achievement or badge is awarded.
scope: Rank tier model: child.rank_tier (1-10 for MVP, e.g., Novice → Expert → Master)
XP thresholds per tier: tier_1 = 0 XP, tier_2 = 50 XP, tier_3 = 100 XP, ... (TBD exact values)
Rank evaluation after XP accumulation: compare total_xp to thresholds, update rank_tier if necessary
Milestone achievement: emit achievement event when tier is reached (owned by BC-05-C4)
Return updated rank and xp_to_next_rank to frontend
Rank is immutable once reached (no tier loss)
acceptance_criteria: Rank thresholds are defined (e.g., tier_1 = 0, tier_2 = 50, tier_3 = 100)
Child's rank tier is updated when XP threshold is crossed
Rank never decreases
xp_to_next_rank is calculated correctly
Achievement event is emitted on rank transition
assumptions: Rank thresholds are simple XP-based milestones (no other conditions)
Max rank tier is 10 (or TBD)
Rank has no gameplay impact in MVP (visual/motivational only)
items.split_rationale: Rank evaluation is distinct from XP accumulation and from achievement awarding; separating allows focused testing.

reasons: Simple threshold comparison and update logic

items.title: Streak tracking system
items.outcome: The system tracks each child's current streak (consecutive days with completed missions) and all-time longest streak; streaks are maintained across sessions.
scope: Streak model: child.current_streak, child.longest_streak (both cumulative day counts)
Streak increment trigger: when child completes a mission on a new day (check if last_activity_date < today)
Streak reset trigger: if no missions for 1 calendar day after last_activity_date, reset current_streak to 0
Background job or check on each attempt: if today != last_activity_date, increment current_streak (or reset)
Longest streak is updated when current_streak exceeds it
Streak bonus: optional, e.g., 3-day streak = +5 XP bonus (TBD, may be owned by BC-04-C4)
non_goals: Reminders or notifications for maintaining streaks
Timezone-aware day calculations (assume UTC for MVP)
acceptance_criteria: Current streak is incremented when mission is completed on new day
Current streak is reset if no missions for 1+ days
Longest streak is updated when current_streak exceeds it
Streak data is persisted and survives session
Streak is displayed in hero profile (BC-02-C6)
assumptions: Day boundary is UTC midnight (or configurable)
Streak logic runs on each attempt submission (no separate job needed)
Streak bonus may be applied here or in BC-04-C4 (TBD)
items.split_rationale: Streak tracking is a distinct gamification concern; separating allows independent testing and potential future features (reminders, notifications).

reasons: Straightforward date comparison and counter logic; may need timezone handling

items.title: Achievement evaluation and awarding logic
items.outcome: The system evaluates achievement unlock conditions after each attempt and awards badges to the child when conditions are met.
scope: Achievement model: id, name (e.g., 'Perfect Score'), condition (rule, e.g., 'score == 10'), badge_icon_url, unlocked_date (if earned)
Achievement conditions: score-based (100% correct), district-based (complete all missions in a district), streak-based (5-day streak), XP-based (reach 500 XP)
Evaluation trigger: after each attempt, check if any achievement conditions are met
Award logic: if condition met and not already awarded, create achievement_earned record and emit event
Achievements cannot be un-awarded (one-time unlock)
Return new achievements in POST /attempt response (optional, or fetch via separate API)
acceptance_criteria: Achievement conditions are defined (score, district, streak, XP)
Evaluation checks all conditions after each attempt
Achievement is awarded when condition is met
Duplicate awards are prevented (unique constraint)
Achievement data includes name, description, icon, unlock date
Achievements are returned in profile API (BC-02-C6)
assumptions: Achievement conditions are rule-based, not event-based (simpler than event system)
Achievements are predefined in DB (no dynamic generation in MVP)
Achievement unlock is permanent and irreversible
items.split_rationale: Achievement logic is distinct from XP, rank, and streak; separating allows focused testing and potential future expansion (social, rarity).

reasons: Rule evaluation and award logic is straightforward; condition checks are simple queries

items.title: Child statistics API and aggregation
items.outcome: The system exposes a child stats API that aggregates and returns missions completed, average score, streak, strongest and weakest districts for display in hero profile and parent dashboard.
scope: GET /api/v1/child/profile endpoint: returns { hero_name, avatar_id, rank_tier, xp_total, current_streak, longest_streak, missions_completed, average_score, strongest_district, weakest_district, achievements }
Aggregations: sum(missions), avg(score per mission), max(strongest_district by avg_score), min(weakest_district by avg_score)
District aggregation: group attempts by district, compute avg score and mission count per district
Caching: optional, cache profile stats for 1 minute to reduce DB queries (not required for MVP)
Deferred computation: stats are computed on-demand (no separate job needed)
non_goals: Real-time stats updates (acceptable lag of 1 second per request)
acceptance_criteria: GET /profile returns all required stats
Missions completed is accurate count
Average score is computed correctly
Streak values are current
Strongest/weakest districts are identified correctly
Achievements array includes all earned badges
assumptions: Stats are computed on-demand (no background aggregation job)
Caching is optional for MVP (add if performance is an issue)
All required data is already in the database (XP, rank, streak, achievements, attempts)
items.split_rationale: Stats aggregation is a distinct API concern; separating allows independent testing and potential caching optimization.

reasons: Query aggregations are straightforward SQL; no complex logic

items.title: Admin authentication and authorization
items.outcome: An admin can log in with email and password and access the admin portal with role-based access control enforced.
scope: Admin login UI: email and password form (leverages BC-01-C4 API)
Middleware: requireRole('admin') on all /api/v1/admin/* routes (owned by BC-01-C8)
Admin session: JWT with admin role stored in memory (like child JWT)
Logout: POST /api/v1/admin/logout, clear JWT, redirect to login
Error handling: 401 for invalid credentials, 403 for unauthorized access
Admin seed script: create 1-2 admin accounts at deployment time (credentials logged or provided)
non_goals: Admin account creation UI (OQ-8 — seed script only)
acceptance_criteria: Admin login form submits email/password
POST /login returns JWT with admin role
Invalid credentials return 401
Unauthorized routes return 403 (checked by middleware)
Logout clears JWT and redirects to login
Admin seed accounts are created at deployment
assumptions: Admin accounts are seeded via script; no self-signup (OQ-8)
JWT is stored in memory (same pattern as child JWT)
items.split_rationale: Admin login UI is specific to the admin portal; separating from general auth logic allows focused admin-specific flow.

reasons: Leverages existing BC-01 auth APIs; standard form and middleware

items.title: Question management interface (CRUD)
items.outcome: An admin can create, read, update, and soft-delete questions with validation and error handling.
scope: Question form: stem (text), answer_options (4 text inputs), correct_answer_idx (dropdown 0-3), topic (dropdown), difficulty (dropdown 1-3)
Create: submit form → POST /api/v1/admin/question → receive question_id
Read: question list table with search/filter by topic, edit/delete buttons
Edit: click edit → populate form with question data → PUT /api/v1/admin/question/:id
Delete: confirm dialog → DELETE /api/v1/admin/question/:id (soft-delete)
Error handling: display validation errors, network errors
Responsive: table on desktop, cards on mobile
non_goals: Rich text editor for question stem
acceptance_criteria: Question form validates all required fields
POST /question creates and returns ID
Question list displays all questions (paginated if >50)
Edit form populates with question data
PUT /question/:id updates and returns success
DELETE /question/:id soft-deletes
Search/filter by topic works
Error messages are displayed
assumptions: Questions are plain text (no rich formatting)
Correct answer is selected via dropdown (0-3)
Topics are predefined (not creatable by admin in MVP)
items.split_rationale: Question management is a distinct admin capability; separating from assessment management allows independent UI/UX development.

reasons: CRUD forms are straightforward; table with pagination and search are standard patterns

items.title: Assessment management interface (CRUD with question assignment)
items.outcome: An admin can create, read, update, and soft-delete assessments and assign questions to them.
scope: Assessment form: name, description, district (dropdown), topic (dropdown), num_questions (auto-filled to 10 for MVP), difficulty (dropdown)
Question selection: multi-select or table of available questions filtered by topic; admin selects 10
Create: submit form → POST /api/v1/admin/assessment → link questions via association table or array
Read: assessment list with name, district, question count, delete button
Edit: click edit → populate form and question selection
Delete: confirm dialog → DELETE /api/v1/admin/assessment/:id (soft-delete)
Responsive: table on desktop, cards on mobile
non_goals: Dynamic question selection based on difficulty
acceptance_criteria: Assessment form validates required fields
POST /assessment creates and links 10 questions
Assessment list displays all assessments
Edit form populates with assessment data and selected questions
PUT /assessment/:id updates and re-links questions
DELETE /assessment/:id soft-deletes
Question count is validated (must be exactly 10 for MVP)
Error messages are displayed
assumptions: Each assessment has exactly 10 questions (fixed for MVP)
Questions are selected from same topic
Admin must manually select 10 questions (no auto-selection algorithm)
items.split_rationale: Assessment management is distinct from question management; separating allows independent testing of the question assignment flow.

reasons: Multi-select UI and question linking are moderately complex; standard form patterns

items.title: User account management (list, view, soft-delete)
items.outcome: An admin can view all parent and child accounts, access account details, and soft-delete accounts if needed.
scope: User list: table with two tabs (Parents, Children)
Parent tab: email, name, num_children, account_created, delete button
Child tab: hero_name, parent_email, rank_tier, missions_completed, last_activity, delete button
User detail view: click user → see full profile (parent email, children list; or child name, parent, stats)
Soft-delete: confirm dialog → DELETE /api/v1/admin/user/:id
Search/filter: by email (parents), by hero_name (children)
Responsive: table on desktop, cards on mobile
acceptance_criteria: User list displays all parents and children
Parent tab shows email, name, num_children
Child tab shows hero_name, parent_email, rank, missions
Search by email (parent) and hero_name (child) works
User detail view shows full profile
DELETE /user/:id soft-deletes account
Deleted users no longer appear in list
Responsive on mobile and desktop
assumptions: Soft-delete is the only deletion strategy (no hard delete in MVP)
Deleted accounts are filtered out of normal list but remain in DB
No partial user data deletion (full account soft-delete only)
items.split_rationale: User account management is a distinct admin concern; separating from content (questions/assessments) allows independent UI/UX.

reasons: Standard list and detail views; search and filter are straightforward

items.title: Subscription and payment overview display
items.outcome: An admin can view subscription status and plan information for all parents to monitor billing readiness.
scope: Subscription dashboard: list or summary of all parent subscriptions
Per parent: email, current plan (free for MVP), subscription_id, renewal_date (if applicable)
For MVP: all parents are on 'free' plan; no Stripe data
Read-only view: no ability to modify subscriptions in MVP
GET /api/v1/admin/subscriptions endpoint returns list
Responsive: table on desktop, cards on mobile
acceptance_criteria: Subscription list displays all parents and their plans
Plan field is accurate (free for MVP)
GET /subscriptions returns list of parent subscriptions
Responsive on mobile and desktop
Read-only (no edit or delete)
assumptions: All MVP users are on 'free' plan (no Stripe data)
Subscription list is read-only for MVP (no admin modifications)
Stripe integration will be handled post-MVP (BC-07)
items.split_rationale: Subscription display is a distinct admin concern; separating allows independent future Stripe integration.

reasons: Simple table display of plan data; read-only for MVP

items.title: Admin portal layout and data-dense UI patterns
items.outcome: The admin portal has a consistent, data-dense layout optimized for desktop with tables, forms, and navigation.
scope: Layout: sidebar navigation, main content area, header with admin name and logout
Navigation: links to Questions, Assessments, Users (Parents/Children), Subscriptions
Data tables: sortable columns, pagination (25 rows per page), search/filter inputs
Forms: two-column layout on desktop for compact display
Colors/typography: professional, not playful (unlike child/parent portals)
Responsive: 768px+ (desktop-first; mobile not required)
Dark mode or light mode: design decision (not required for MVP)
Error handling: toast notifications or alerts for errors
acceptance_criteria: Navigation sidebar is present with all main sections
Tables display data with sortable columns and pagination
Search/filter inputs are functional
Forms use two-column layout on desktop
Responsive at 768px+ (no mobile responsiveness required)
Error messages are displayed clearly
Admin name and logout are visible in header
assumptions: Admin portal is desktop-only (768px+); mobile support not required
Data-dense layout is acceptable (no whitespace padding like child portal)
Professional styling is preferred over playful superhero theme
items.split_rationale: Portal layout and UI patterns are cross-cutting; treating as a separate capability ensures consistent design across all admin pages.

reasons: Applies to all 5 admin capabilities; should be established early and validated across all views
May require multiple design iterations and accessibility reviews

items.title: Project structure and monorepo layout (client + server)
items.outcome: The project is organized as a monorepo with /client (Vite SPA) and /server (Express API) directories; builds are independent and deployment is straightforward.
scope: Root structure: /client, /server, /shared (optional), package.json (workspace root), .env.example
Client: /src (React + TS), /public, vite.config.ts, package.json (dependencies)
Server: /src (Express + TS), /migrations, /seeds, server.ts, package.json (dependencies)
Root package.json: workspaces (npm/yarn) or workspace setting, scripts for build/dev/test
Scripts: npm run build (builds both), npm run dev (runs both in parallel or serial), npm run start (production)
Version control: .gitignore excludes /node_modules, /dist, /build, .env (but not .env.example)
TypeScript config: separate tsconfig for client and server; root tsconfig extends base
non_goals: Polyrepo with separate repos for client and server
Shared code between client and server (separate node_modules)
Workspace packages (all code in client/server, not separate packages)
acceptance_criteria: Monorepo structure created with /client and /server
Both directories have independent package.json and build configs
Root scripts (build, dev, start) work correctly
TypeScript configs are separate and correct
.gitignore excludes build artifacts and node_modules
Workspace tooling (npm/yarn) is configured
assumptions: npm workspaces is the preferred tool (not yarn or lerna)
Client and server are deployed together (single server);
items.split_rationale: Project structure is foundational; separating as a capability ensures it's completed before other work begins.

reasons: Standard monorepo setup; straightforward directory layout and script configuration

items.title: Express static serving and SPA fallback routing
items.outcome: Express serves the built Vite SPA from /client/dist and provides SPA fallback routing so all non-/api/* routes return index.html.
scope: Express middleware stack: static file serving, API routing, SPA fallback
Serve static files: app.use(express.static('client/dist'))
API routing: app.use('/api', apiRouter) — passes through to API routes
SPA fallback: app.use((req, res) => res.sendFile('client/dist/index.html')) — catches all other routes
Content-type headers: set correctly for .js, .css, .html files
Gzip compression: optional, enables for static assets
CORS: configure if needed (likely not for single-domain deployment)
non_goals: CDN or caching headers (simple Cache-Control only)
acceptance_criteria: Express serves /client/dist as root
/api/* routes pass through to API handlers
Non-/api/* routes return index.html (SPA routing)
Static files have correct Content-Type headers
CSS and JS files load correctly in browser
SPA routes like /child/mission/:id work (return index.html)
assumptions: Single Express process serves both API and static assets
Vite builds to /client/dist for production
SPA fallback is sufficient for single-domain deployment
items.split_rationale: Static serving and SPA fallback are distinct infrastructure concerns; separating allows focused testing of routing and file serving.

reasons: Straightforward Express middleware; standard SPA fallback pattern

items.title: Vite development server configuration and API proxying
items.outcome: Vite dev server is configured to proxy /api/* requests to the Express backend during local development.
scope: vite.config.ts: configure server.proxy to route /api/* to http://localhost:3000 (or configurable port)
Proxy configuration: rewrite path if needed (e.g., strip prefix), changeOrigin for CORS
Dev server port: Vite on 5173 (default), Express on 3000 (configurable via PORT env var)
Environment variables: VITE_API_URL (exposed to client-side) for API calls
npm run dev: script to start both Vite and Express concurrently (or serially, design decision)
non_goals: Hot module reloading (HMR) advanced config (Vite default is sufficient)
acceptance_criteria: Vite dev server starts on port 5173
Express backend starts on port 3000
/api/* requests are proxied from Vite to Express
API responses are received correctly by frontend
npm run dev starts both servers
assumptions: Vite port 5173, Express port 3000 (standard defaults)
npm concurrently or similar tool is used for parallel startup
Proxy is simple HTTP (no special WebSocket handling needed in MVP)
items.split_rationale: Dev server configuration is a distinct developer experience concern; separating allows independent setup and testing.

reasons: Standard Vite config; simple proxy setup

items.title: SQLite schema migrations and versioning system
items.outcome: The database uses versioned migration files that run automatically on app startup, ensuring schema consistency across deployments.
scope: Migration directory: /server/migrations/ with files like 001_init.sql, 002_add_streak.sql, etc.
Migration runner: Node script or library (e.g., sql.js or custom runner) that tracks applied migrations
Migration tracking: _migrations table stores { id, filename, applied_at }
On startup: app reads migration directory, compares to _migrations table, applies new migrations in order
Rollback strategy: for MVP, no rollback needed (forward-only migrations)
Migration content: CREATE TABLE, ALTER TABLE, CREATE INDEX statements (no data manipulation in migrations)
non_goals: Data migration or seed data (separate from schema migrations)
acceptance_criteria: Migration files are created with sequential naming (001, 002, etc.)
Migration runner reads and applies migrations on startup
_migrations table tracks applied migrations
Duplicate migrations are skipped (idempotent)
New migrations are applied without errors
Schema is consistent across deployments
assumptions: Migrations are forward-only (no rollback in MVP)
Migration runner is synchronous (runs before app starts)
All schema changes are in migrations (no manual SQL)
items.split_rationale: Schema versioning is a distinct data layer concern; separating allows focused testing of migration logic.

reasons: Standard migration pattern; implementation is straightforward

items.title: Database seed script with idempotent data loading
items.outcome: An idempotent seed script populates the database with initial data (subjects, topics, assessments, questions, admin accounts) on deployment.
scope: Seed script: /server/seeds/seed.js (or similar)
Data to seed: 1 subject (Mathropolis), 5 topics (Math, Science, History, Art, PE), 15 assessments (3 per district), 150 questions, 1-2 admin accounts
Idempotency: check if data exists before inserting (e.g., IF NOT EXISTS pattern or unique constraints)
Execution: npm run seed (manual) or automatic on first startup (design decision)
Error handling: log warnings if duplicates are encountered, continue with seed
Admin credentials: logged to console or written to .env.local (not committed)
non_goals: Test data generation (fixtures for tests are separate)
Data deletion or reset (no truncate in seed script)
acceptance_criteria: Seed script inserts subjects, topics, assessments, questions, admin accounts
150 questions are inserted (or verified if already present)
Admin accounts are created (or verified if already present)
Seed is idempotent (running twice does not duplicate data)
npm run seed executes without errors
Data is queryable after seed completes
assumptions: Seed data is provided as JSON or embedded in seed script (not CSV import)
Idempotency is achieved via unique constraints or EXISTS checks
Admin credentials are logged (not saved to repo)
items.split_rationale: Seed data loading is a distinct data initialization concern; separating allows independent testing and data management.

reasons: Data is large (150 questions), but insertion logic is straightforward

items.title: SQLite WAL mode and performance tuning
items.outcome: SQLite is configured with WAL mode for better concurrency and performance; pragmas are set for safe, fast operation.
scope: WAL mode: PRAGMA journal_mode = WAL
Synchronous: PRAGMA synchronous = NORMAL (safer than OFF, faster than FULL)
Busy timeout: PRAGMA busy_timeout = 5000 (5 seconds, allows concurrent writes to retry)
Foreign keys: PRAGMA foreign_keys = ON (enforce referential integrity)
Temp store: PRAGMA temp_store = MEMORY (optional, improve performance)
Applied on: app startup or in migration file
Verification: query PRAGMA journal_mode and confirm WAL
non_goals: Advanced SQLite tuning (index optimization is separate)
acceptance_criteria: WAL mode is enabled
Synchronous is set to NORMAL
Busy timeout is 5 seconds
Foreign keys are enforced
App starts without errors
PRAGMA journal_mode returns WAL
assumptions: Single-server deployment; WAL mode is sufficient (no multi-process scaling needed)
Pragmas are set on app startup or in migration (not manually each session)
items.split_rationale: SQLite tuning is a distinct performance concern; separating allows focused testing and optimization.

reasons: Simple pragma configuration; minimal code

items.title: Process supervision with PM2 or systemd
items.outcome: The Node.js process is supervised and automatically restarted on crash; app stays up even if errors occur.
scope: Process manager: PM2 (recommended for MVP simplicity) or systemd (for VPS deployment)
PM2 setup: npm run build && pm2 start server.js --name hero-academy
systemd setup: /etc/systemd/system/hero-academy.service file with ExecStart, Restart=always
Logs: PM2 logs or systemd journal (journalctl -u hero-academy -f)
Monitoring: PM2 monit or systemd status (optional for MVP)
Configuration: ecosystem.config.js (PM2) or .service file (systemd)
non_goals: Docker containerization (deployment target is VPS, not Kubernetes)
acceptance_criteria: Process manager is installed and configured
App starts successfully with process manager
If app crashes, it automatically restarts
Logs are accessible (PM2 or systemd journal)
Status command shows app is running
assumptions: Deployment target is VPS or similar (not container)
PM2 or systemd is available on target machine
Single process (no clustering in MVP)
items.split_rationale: Process supervision is a distinct operations concern; separating allows independent setup based on deployment target.

reasons: Standard process manager setup; straightforward configuration

items.title: Nightly backup and data protection strategy
items.outcome: SQLite database file is backed up nightly to object storage (AWS S3, GCS, or local backup); data loss risk is minimized.
scope: Backup script: cron job or systemd timer that runs nightly (e.g., 2 AM UTC)
Backup destination: AWS S3, Google Cloud Storage, or local NAS (TBD based on OQ-2 deployment target)
Backup content: database.sqlite and WAL files (if any)
Retention: keep last 7 days of backups (configurable)
Verification: log backup success/failure, alert on failure (simple email or webhook)
Recovery procedure: documented steps to restore from backup (not automated in MVP)
acceptance_criteria: Backup script is created and scheduled
Backup job runs nightly without errors
Backup files are uploaded to storage
Multiple backups are retained (7-day history)
Success/failure is logged
Recovery procedure is documented
assumptions: Backup target is determined by OQ-2 (deployment environment decision)
Nightly backups are sufficient (no real-time backup needed for MVP)
Manual recovery is acceptable (no automated failover)
items.split_rationale: Backup strategy is a distinct data protection concern; separating allows independent setup and verification.

reasons: Standard backup pattern; implementation depends on backup target

items.title: Environment configuration and dotenv setup
items.outcome: Environment variables are managed via dotenv for local development and environment-specific configuration is applied on deployment.
scope: .env.example: template with all required variables (committed to repo)
.env: local development settings (gitignored, created by running cp .env.example .env)
.env.production: production settings (not committed; applied on deploy)
Variables: DB_PATH, PORT, NODE_ENV, API_URL, JWT_SECRET, ADMIN_EMAIL, ADMIN_PASSWORD, etc.
Loading: dotenv package in server.js on startup
Client-side: VITE_API_URL and similar (prefixed with VITE_) for Vite to expose
Validation: check required variables on startup, error if missing
non_goals: Secrets manager (AWS Secrets Manager, Vault) — dotenv is sufficient for MVP
Config file in YAML or TOML (dotenv is sufficient)
acceptance_criteria: .env.example lists all required variables
.env (or .env.local) is gitignored
dotenv loads variables on startup
APP validates required variables and errors if missing
DB_PATH, PORT, NODE_ENV, JWT_SECRET are configurable
Client-side API URL is configurable via VITE_API_URL
assumptions: dotenv is the dependency for loading .env files
Client-side Vite exposes VITE_* prefixed variables automatically
Production .env is provided by deployment process (not committed)
items.split_rationale: Environment configuration is foundational for all environments; separating ensures it's established early.

reasons: Simple dotenv setup; straightforward variable management
